docs.title: Talos-SDL Documentation
docs.text:
Welcome to the documentation for the Talos-SDL Orchestrator – an open-source, agentic host framework for Self-Driving Laboratories (SDLs) and autonomous experimentation.

This documentation serves as your guide to understanding, operating, and extending Talos-SDL, designed to empower researchers to rapidly build bespoke robotic instruments, integrate custom sensors, and execute AI-orchestrated workflows. Talos-SDL is designed with rapid prototyping and deployment in mind and targets single users and small groups interested in exploring agentic scientific laboratory instrumentation.

Much of this documentation is in draft form and has been written by AI based on the codebase and journals the developer has kept during the framework design process. Revisions and review are in process.

docs.title: Documentation Sections
docs.text:
Explore the different facets of Talos-SDL:

  • Tutorials: Step-by-step guides for users to operate the system and for new developers to get started with firmware creation.
  • Concepts: High-level overviews of the project's core philosophy, features, and overall architectural design.
  • Developer Guides: Practical guides, best practices, and templates for developers looking to extend, modify, or contribute to the Talos-SDL framework.
  • Design Protocols: Formal specifications and normative guidelines for how various components and systems within Talos-SDL are designed and expected to behave.
  • API Reference: Technical documentation automatically generated from the Python source code, detailing classes, methods, and functions.

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
The repository is organized to separate the high-level AI logic from the low-level hardware control, ensuring modularity and maintainability:

  • host/: The "Brain." Contains the Python application that runs on your PC, including AI agents, graphical user interfaces, and device management logic.
  • firmware/: The "Hands." Contains the CircuitPython code that runs on your microcontrollers, tailored for specific instruments like the sidekick robotic arm or colorimeter.
  • communicate/: The "Language." Contains the abstracted classes and code to ensure communication between host, firmware, and other devices follow well-formatted constructs.
  • shared_lib/: Common protocols, utilities, and message definitions that ensure consistent communication and behavior across both host and firmware.
  • docs/: This documentation! Containing all guides, concepts, protocols, and API references.

docs.title: Agentic Chat Loop
docs.text: Status: Currently planner.py generates a static JSON plan from a prompt. You want a conversation. Goal: A Read-Eval-Print Loop (REPL) where you chat, and the AI executes tools immediately.

docs.title: Initial thoughts
docs.text:
1. Refactor planner.py: Instead of generating a full list [...], switch the system prompt to "ReAct" (Reasoning + Acting) style.
  2. The Loop (Host Side):
    • User types "Dispense 100ul into A1".
    • LLM sees tools (sidekick, colorimeter).
    • LLM output: {"tool": "sidekick", "cmd": "dispense_at", "args": {...}}.
    • Host executes immediately via DeviceManager.
    • Host feeds result (SUCCESS or DATA_RESPONSE) back to LLM context.
    • LLM replies to user: "Done. What next?"
  3. Integration: Create host/ai/ai_chat.py which unifies ai_plan.py and ai_execute.py into this single loop.
  4. Remove LLM ties: host/ai/vertext_agent.py is currently tied to a single LLM provider which makes it difficult to use other such as Ollama for local serving of the LLM. It might be better to implement a "Postman Pattern" similar to what is in communicate/ to resovle.
    • Create a BaseAgent abstract class
    • Implement VertextAgent and OllamaAgent subclasses
    • The Agent class should just be a wrapper that takes params dictionary e.g. {"provider": "ollama", "model": "llama3"} to decide which backend to use. Implementing the OpenAI API format and targeting OpenAI-compatible will simplify this part of the project.
  5. MCP Integration: Implementing a standard that allows the AI to discover tools. MCP will likely make the current task - an agentic chat loop - obsolete; however the MCP server would require significant overhead to get the features operational. We want to address the chat loop first using existing infrastructure and start standardizing tool definitions with clean descriptions and args schema such as those in sidekick/__init__.py.

docs.title: Stage 1: Abstract the LLM Postman
docs.text:
• Refactor vertext_agent.py into a provider-agnostic llm_manager.py.
  • Utilize a strategy similar to the Postman Pattern in communicate and generate a BaseAgent abstract class with VertextAgent and OllamaAgent subclasses.
    • Avoid the use of ABC and abstractmethod in the abc library, which is not part of circuitpython and therefore incompatible with the hardware.
    • Implement OpenAI API format where possible to target OpenAI compatible solutions.
  • Success condition: Can swap between Gemini and a local Ollama instance.

docs.title: Stage 2: The "Stateful Tool user" (Logic)
docs.text:
• Refactor planner.py logic to handle single-turn execution
  • Modify PlateManager to be able to "undo" or "verify" state if a hardware command fails (received a PROBLEM message).
  • Success Condition: The system can successfully execute a conditional multi-step task where the second step is generated only after the first step's result is processed.
    • Test Scenario: A script is given the prompt: "Add 100µL to well A1. If and only if that succeeds, measure the spectrum."
    • Validation: The logs must show the sidekick command executing, the DeviceManager receiving a SUCCESS message, the PlateManager updating the volume of A1, and then the LLM receiving that confirmation as context before it generates the colorimeter command.

docs.title: Stage 3: The "REPL" (Interaction)
docs.text:
• Create host/ai/ai_chat.py
  • The Loop:
    • User Input ->
    • LLM Reasoning ->
    • Tool Call Proposal ->
    • Human Confirmation ->
    • DeviceManager execution ->
    • Parse DATA_RESPONSE ->
    • LLM summarizes result to User.
  • Success Condition: A continuous, conversational session where the AI maintains contextual memory across multiple human interventions and device responses.
    • Test Scenario:
      1. User: "Move the arm to A1." (AI moves arm).
      2. User: "What's the current position?" (AI calculates world coordinates via get_info and answers).
      3. User: "Dispense 50µL of red reagent here." (AI identifies "here" as A1 and identifies "red" as Pump 1 from the World Model, then executes).
    • Validation: The AI must correctly resolve "here" and "red" based on the ongoing conversation and the PlateManager state without the user repeating the coordinates or pump IDs.

docs.title: Next project: Hardware-as-a-Service (MCP)
docs.text:
Upon completing and validating the agentic chat loop, we will move on to standardizing the lab for the wider AI ecosystem. This branch will need additional planning, but the initial ideas are the following

  • Wrap the DeviceManager in an MCP server. This allows the lab to be "plugged in" to any modern agentic framework without writing any more custom chat code.
  • Success Condition: An external, off-the-shelf MCP client (e.g., Claude Desktop, mcp-inspector, or an IDE-based agent) can automatically discover and control the laboratory instruments without any custom ALIF-specific integration code.
    • Test Scenario: Point an MCP-compliant client at the ALIF MCP server.
    • Validation: The client automatically populates its "Toolbox" with entries like sidekick_move_to and colorimeter_measure (derived from your handlers.py documentation), and a command triggered from the external client successfully moves physical hardware.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
Beginning with step 1.

After much vibe coding, we are now at a point where test_agent_stage2.py demonstrates agentic control of a single multi-step command. In the example, the sidekick is homed, it moves to well A1 to dispense liquid and then measures the spectrum. The functionality required some updates to firmware (SYSTEM_CONFIG) to provide ai_guidance on how to use the hardware. There is likely a more elegant way to provide this manual but the current approach demonstrates a self-documenting instrument.

The entire transaction is logged for later review. Immediately it can be seen that chat history will grow quite significantly with more complex operations. I don't know how this type of issue is handled in chatbots.

We have successfully abstracted the LLM, making it possible to use the llm of choice. Using gemini-2.5-flash-lite is presently inexpensive and functional.

The ai_guidance currently intertwines the sidekick and the colorimeter. That is unavoidable in my test system, but will need to be thought through in a future version.

We have successfully established the foundational logic and communication plumbing for the agentic laboratory.

docs.title: Summary of Completion
docs.text:
1. Stage 1: LLM Abstraction (The "LLM Postman"):

    • Refactored the AI architecture into a provider-agnostic system.
    • Implemented BaseAgent (hardware-compatible, no abc library), VertexAgent (for Gemini), and OpenAIAgent (for Ollama/OpenAI-compatible local models).
    • Created LLMManager to act as a factory, allowing you to swap between cloud and local AI via a single environment variable.

  2. Stage 2: The ReAct Logic (The "Reasoning Engine"):

    • Dynamic Guidance: Updated firmware and ai_utils to pull "Operational Metadata" (ai_guidance) directly from the hardware. The AI now knows it must home before moving and center the sensor before measuring.
    • Iterative Execution: Created the AgentExecutor to run a ReAct loop. Instead of generating a static batch plan, the system now sends one command, waits for the hardware response, and feeds that "observation" back to the LLM to decide the next step.
    • Stateful Memory: Integrated the PlateManager so the AI maintains a "mental map" of well contents across steps.
    • Telemetry & Budgeting: Implemented real-time token tracking and cumulative cost estimation for the Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite model.
    • Flight Data Recorder: Built a logging system that saves every transaction (System Context, Prompts, JSON responses, and Hardware Observations) into timestamped JSON files in the temp/ directory for audit and debugging.

docs.title: What Needs to Be Done Next: Stage 3
docs.text:
We are currently at the threshold of the final stage: The Agentic REPL (Conversation Loop).

The Goal: Move from a script that executes one specific goal and exits to a persistent "Laboratory Cockpit" session.

Next Steps: 1. Develop ai_chat.py: Create a command-line interface that allows for a continuous back-and-forth conversation. 2. Session Persistence: Ensure the Agent history and PlateManager state persist across multiple distinct goals (e.g., Goal 1: "Home the system" -> Goal 2: "Prepare a 1:1 mixture in B2"). 3. Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) Safety: Implement a gate where the user can review and approve the AI’s proposed JSON command before it is dispatched to the physical hardware. 4. Refined "Completion" Logic: Adapt the COMPLETE status so it returns control to the human user rather than shutting down the DeviceManager.

The last few steps took one more prompt, we now have a REPL that can do basic movements of the sidekick :)

docs.title: Continuing with the agentic loop
docs.text:
With a larger hard drive, I can install ollama and test local models. The computer is far to low-end to run anything with decent speed, however, so this will need to be put on hold for a future project. I will need to continue with vertex.

Some tasks: - modify the loop so that the user is not asked to approve each step in a sequence - Provide sequence editing capabilities - Test on a real sample

I've fixed - perhaps - some issues with the colorimeter not exposing the settings and the chat log storing information from a DATA_RESPONSE. However, I am hitting quotas for vertex AI pretty frequently and I don't know how to resolve that issue.

The issue was possibly resolved by moving to the API key via Gemini instead of using vertex. Industry-standard may not be viable since the system is simply way too confusing.

Progress - planning for a multi-agent style chat that can both run and analyze data. Draft PRD

Below is an edited but largely AI-generated PRD for completing the agentic chat loop. IMO there is far more sophistication here than is necessary for version 1. Modifying this to provide a solid working example of the /run and /data modes will be sufficient.

docs.title: Project Requirements Document
docs.text: Project: ALIF Agentic Laboratory Cockpit – Refactored Chat Loop Version: 1.0 (Final)

docs.title: 1. Executive Summary
docs.text:
Refactor the chat loop to: - Support firmware‑resident deterministic skills (e.g., optimize_gain) that encapsulate closed‑loop calibration/optimization on the instrument. - Introduce explicit, user‑controlled modes: /run (instrument control; strict JSON plans) and /data (analysis; no device I/O). - Use a single LLM agent with mode‑specific contexts and separate histories, plus a temporary one‑shot analysis in /run that summarizes DATA_RESPONSE without changing modes.

Design must anticipate future features (multi‑agent routing, control DSL, reflexive planning) without increasing complexity for v1.

docs.title: 2. Goals & Objectives
docs.text:
• G1: Deterministic, firmware‑driven procedures callable through a stable JSON planning interface.
  • G2: Explicit, intentional mode switching via slash commands to prevent accidental actuation.
  • G3: Single agent, multiple contexts (RUN/DATA), isolated histories; temporary, no‑history one‑shot analysis in /run.
  • G4: Preserve the existing JSON plan schema; no host‑side DSL in v1.
  • G5: Strong logging/observability, dataset registry, and artifact lineage.
  • G6: Architecture ready for future multi‑agent routing and control DSL.

docs.title: 3. In‑Scope (v1)
docs.text:
• Firmware skills integration (host invokes skills as normal commands; instrument performs closed‑loop logic).
  • /run and /data modes with explicit user control.
  • Single agent with mode‑specific system contexts and histories.
  • Plan review gate in /run (confirm/edit/reject).
  • One‑shot analysis in /run upon DATA_RESPONSE (no mode switch).
  • Minimal analysis tools in /data (list/summarize/plot).
  • Dataset registry and session logging.
  • Safety: timeouts, validation, dry‑run.

docs.title: 4. Out‑of‑Scope (v1)
docs.text:
• Host‑side control DSL (loops/ifs).
  • Reflexive multi‑turn planning with observation between each step.
  • On‑device skill authoring from host.
  • Advanced analytics beyond summarize/plot.
  • Cloud/remote multi‑user services.

docs.title: 5. Stakeholders & Roles
docs.text:
• Product/PI: Scientific goals, acceptance criteria.
  • Firmware Team: FSM skills, capability schema, deterministic routines.
  • Host Team: REPL, Planner/Executor, modes, logging, analysis tools.
  • Operators/Researchers: Use /run and /data; provide usability and safety feedback.

docs.title: 6. Key Assumptions
docs.text:
• Firmware exposes self‑documenting capabilities/skills via structured help (DATA_RESPONSE) including args, constraints, and return schema.
  • Instruments are deterministic; closed‑loop logic resides on the device.
  • DeviceManager and message protocol remain stable.
  • Single LLM agent suffices for v1; context isolation is feasible.

docs.title: 7.1 Mode System (REPL & Routing)
docs.text:
• FR‑RUN‑1: /run activates instrument‑control mode; agent responses must be JSON plans only.
  • FR‑RUN‑2: /run enforces plan review: run/y, reject/n, del <#>, edit <#>.
  • FR‑RUN‑3: /run prohibits analysis tools except one‑shot summaries (see 7.4.1).
  • FR‑DATA‑1: /data activates analysis mode; no device I/O permitted.
  • FR‑DATA‑2: /data allows natural‑language replies and use of analysis tools.
  • FR‑MODE‑UX: Provide /mode, /status, /help with clear state feedback.

docs.title: 7.2 Firmware Skills (Closed‑Loop Procedures)
docs.text:
• FR‑SKILL‑1: Host enumerates device skills from the capability/help payload at startup.
  • FR‑SKILL‑2: Skills are invoked as normal commands in plans:
    • Example:
      • { "device": "colorimeter", "command": "optimize_gain", "args": { "channel": "yellow", "target_pct": 98, "backoff_levels": 1 } }
  • FR‑SKILL‑3: Skills return structured DATA_RESPONSE results (e.g., final_gain, iterations, trace).
  • FR‑SKILL‑4: Host validates args against the device capability schema; rejects invalid plans at review.

docs.title: 7.3 Planner / Agent Behavior
docs.text:
• FR‑PLAN‑1: Planner RUN context lists available commands/skills, operational guidance, world model, and strict JSON rules.
  • FR‑PLAN‑2: Planner leverages world model safety constraints (e.g., volume caps).
  • FR‑AGT‑1: Single agent with two contexts and two isolated histories (/run and /data).
  • FR‑AGT‑2: Switching modes must not leak history; the system switches system prompts and uses the appropriate history.

docs.title: 7.4.1 One‑Shot Analysis in /run (NEW)
docs.text:
• FR‑RUN‑ANALYSIS‑1: When a DATA_RESPONSE is received in /run, the system invokes a temporary analysis context using the same agent, with:
  • Short, deterministic system instruction.
  • No device‑control affordances.
  • No history (does not append to /run or /data histories).
  • Input template: - The user asked: "" I executed: .() I received: Summarize concisely and answer the user’s question.
  • FR‑RUN‑ANALYSIS‑2: Output a human‑readable summary (e.g., “LED intensity is 4 mA; LED is off.”) and remain in /run.
  • FR‑RUN‑ANALYSIS‑3: The temporary analysis must never emit device commands or plan JSON.
  • FR‑RUN‑ANALYSIS‑4: Print a hint: “Type /data for deeper analysis or /explain last to revisit this summary.”
  • FR‑RUN‑ANALYSIS‑5: Store the raw DATA_RESPONSE and the generated summary for recall.

docs.title: 7.4.2 Minimal Analysis Tools in /data
docs.text:
• FR‑DATA‑TOOLS: Provide tools:
  • list_datasets()
  • describe(id)
  • summarize(id, columns)
  • plot(id, x, y, kind)
  • FR‑DATA‑EXEC: Run tools in a constrained sandbox; save artifacts to temp/analysis and temp/plots.
  • FR‑DATA‑NOACT: /data forbids any device I/O.

docs.title: 7.5 Dataset Registry & Artifacts
docs.text:
• FR‑DS‑1: Register datasets on each DATA_RESPONSE (and analysis outputs) with:
  • id, path, schema, origin_device, origin_command, timestamp, tags.
  • FR‑DS‑2: Provide /datasets and /artifacts commands to browse.

docs.title: 7.6 Logging & Observability
docs.text:
• FR‑LOG‑1: Extend session_history with: session start, mode switches, plans, confirmations, step results, datasets, artifacts, errors.
  • FR‑LOG‑2: executor.save_log() writes JSON to temp/chat-session-*.json.
  • FR‑LOG‑3: For skills, capture and store iteration traces returned by firmware.
  • FR‑LOG‑4: Log one‑shot summaries tagged as "analysis_reply": "one_shot" and link to the originating DATA_RESPONSE.
  • FR‑LOG‑5: Store raw DATA_RESPONSE for /last and /explain last.

docs.title: 7.7 Safety & Guardrails
docs.text:
• FR‑SAFE‑1: Timeouts and retries on device comms; safe aborts.
  • FR‑SAFE‑2: Strict validation: unknown device/command/args → reject at review.
  • FR‑SAFE‑3: /data enforces no device actions at routing and execution layers.
  • FR‑SAFE‑4: Default dry‑run option in /run (configurable).
  • FR‑SAFE‑5: Temporary analysis in /run is sandboxed to prohibit device commands.
  • FR‑SAFE‑6: If the question cannot be answered from the payload, the summary must say so and suggest /data.

docs.title: 7.8 Error Handling
docs.text:
• FR‑ERR‑1: Non‑JSON LLM output in /run → treated as completion/refusal; no actuation.
  • FR‑ERR‑2: Device errors → clear message, abort safely.
  • FR‑ERR‑3: /data tool errors return structured messages without terminating the session.

docs.title: 7.9 Performance
docs.text: • FR‑PERF‑1: Incremental logging to avoid data loss.

docs.title: 7.10 UX Commands (REPL)
docs.text:
• /run, /data, /mode, /status, /help, /clear, /log, /datasets, /artifacts, /dryrun on|off
  • /explain last (replay last one‑shot summary; no I/O), /last (print raw last DATA_RESPONSE), /summary (recap: last command, last dataset, last summary).

docs.title: 8. Non‑Functional Requirements
docs.text:
• Reliability: No device action in /data; retries/timeouts on I/O.
  • Safety: Human‑in‑the‑loop by default in /run.
  • Maintainability: Adding firmware skills does not require host code changes; auto‑discovered via schema.
  • Extensibility: Clear interfaces for future router, DSL, and multi‑agent support.
  • Security: No external network execution; analysis sandbox without external I/O.
  • Portability: Cross‑platform compatible with current environment.

docs.title: 9.1 Capability/Help Schema (Device → Host)
docs.text:
{
  "metadata": {
    "device": "colorimeter",
    "ai_guidance": "Prefer device skills for calibration; avoid host loops."
  },
  "data": {
    "measure_spectrum": {
      "ai_enabled": true,
      "args": { "avg": { "type": "int", "min": 1, "max": 10 } }
    },
    "optimize_gain": {
      "ai_enabled": true,
      "args": {
        "channel": { "type": "enum", "values": ["yellow","red","green","blue"] },
        "target_pct": { "type": "number", "min": 50, "max": 100, "default": 98 },
        "backoff_levels": { "type": "int", "min": 0, "max": 3, "default": 1 },
        "max_iters": { "type": "int", "min": 1, "max": 16, "default": 8 }
      },
      "returns": { "final_gain": "int", "iterations": "int", "saturated": "bool", "trace": "array" }
    }
  }
}

docs.title: 9.2 Plan (Host → Device)
docs.text:
{
  "plan": [
    {
      "device": "colorimeter",
      "command": "optimize_gain",
      "args": { "channel": "yellow", "target_pct": 98, "backoff_levels": 1 }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: 9.3 Skill Result (Device → Host)
docs.text:
{
  "status": "DATA_RESPONSE",
  "payload": {
    "data": {
      "final_gain": 5,
      "iterations": 4,
      "saturated": false,
      "trace": [
        { "gain": 2, "peak": 0.42 },
        { "gain": 3, "peak": 0.71 },
        { "gain": 4, "peak": 0.89 },
        { "gain": 5, "peak": 0.97 }
      ]
    }
  }
}

docs.title: 9.4 Dataset Registry Entry
docs.text:
{
  "id": "ds_20260217_1139_01",
  "path": "temp/data/ds_20260217_1139_01.csv",
  "schema": ["wavelength_nm","yellow","red","green","blue"],
  "origin_device": "colorimeter",
  "origin_command": "measure_spectrum",
  "timestamp": "2026-02-17T16:39:12Z",
  "tags": ["spectrum","post_optimize_gain"]
}

docs.title: 10. Architecture Overview (v1)
docs.text:
• REPL (chat.py): Parses slash commands; manages current mode; displays status/help. Delegates to AgentExecutor for /run and Analysis tools for /data. Provides /explain last, /last, /summary.
  • Planner: Builds mode‑specific system prompts. RUN prompt enumerates commands/skills and strict JSON response rules.
  • Agent (single): One instance, two contexts, two histories. Temporary one‑shot analysis in /run uses the same agent with no history and no device tools.
  • AgentExecutor: Validates plans; plan review; sends commands; waits for results; updates logs, plate state, and dataset registry; triggers one‑shot analysis on DATA_RESPONSE.
  • Analysis Tools: Minimal local toolkit for /data; no device access.
  • DeviceManager: Unchanged serial/message I/O.
  • Firmware: FSM with self‑documented skills; deterministic routines.

docs.title: /Run Mode
docs.text:
• Agent outputs JSON‑only; non‑JSON handled gracefully (no actuation).
  • Human can review/edit/reject plans.
  • Skill invocation (e.g., optimize_gain) completes with structured results logged.
  • On DATA_RESPONSE, a natural‑language one‑shot summary is printed; mode remains /run.

docs.title: /Data Mode
docs.text:
• No device commands are issued.
  • list_datasets, summarize, plot operate on sample datasets; artifacts saved and listed.

docs.title: General
docs.text:
• /mode, /status, /help reflect state.
  • save_log includes plans, steps, datasets, artifacts, one‑shot summaries, errors.

docs.title: 12. Risks & Mitigations
docs.text:
• R1: Firmware bottleneck for new skills. Mitigation: Clear skill schema; prioritize a core set; version skills.
  • R2: Context leakage between modes. Mitigation: Separate histories; hard prompt switch; no‑history temporary analyses.
  • R3: LLM fabricates commands. Mitigation: Validate strictly against discovered capability schema; review gate.
  • R4: User confusion about modes. Mitigation: Prominent status; /mode echo; /help; hints after one‑shot summaries.
  • R5: Analysis scope creep. Mitigation: Minimal toolset in v1; stable extensible interfaces.

docs.title: 13. Deliverables
docs.text:
• Updated REPL with slash commands, status, and one‑shot summary helpers.
  • Revised Planner with two contexts.
  • AgentExecutor changes: skill‑aware validation, one‑shot analysis trigger, enhanced logging.
  • Minimal Analysis Tools and Dataset Registry.
  • Docs: user guide, developer guide, capability/skill schema reference.
  • Test plan and simulated device scripts.

docs.title: 14. Milestones & Timeline (Indicative)
docs.text:
• M1 – Design freeze (schemas, prompts, modes):
  • M2 – REPL + modes + contexts:
  • M3 – Skill integration + plan review hardening:
  • M4 – Dataset registry + minimal analysis tools:
  • M5 – Logging/observability + docs + tests:
  • M6 – Pilot & feedback:

docs.title: 15. Future Direction (Not in v1)
docs.text:
• Multi‑agent router: PlannerAgent (/run) + AnalystAgent (/data) with explicit overrides.
  • Control DSL: Host‑side structured plans (LOOP/IF/UNTIL/OBSERVE) and variable store.
  • Reflexive planning: Iterative stepwise plans with intermediate observations.
  • Richer analytics: Fitting, calibration curves, reports.
  • Policy engine: Safety policies and authorization.

docs.title: 16. Dependencies
docs.text:
• Stable DeviceManager and message protocol.
  • Firmware emitting structured, self‑documented capabilities and skills.
  • LLM provider availability/config.
  • Local filesystem for temp/ logging and artifacts.

docs.title: 17. Glossary
docs.text:
• Skill: Firmware‑resident deterministic routine (e.g., optimize_gain) exposed as a command.
  • /run: Instrument control mode; strict JSON plans; one‑shot summaries allowed.
  • /data: Analysis mode; no device I/O.
  • Dataset Registry: Index of datasets with path, schema, provenance, tags.
  • Planner: Builds system prompt/context; constrains model outputs by mode.

docs.title: Acl 260219
docs.text:
Focus today is on the user experience.

  • The /run mode responds well to sidekick and colorimeter commands. In one instance, I asked the agent to measure from five wells - the first plan only returned for and I rejected it, the second iteration contained all five.
  • The data is preserved, but it is not organized well. Both CSV and JSON files are stored - not sure if that is useful. More importantly, there is insufficient information in the dataset to tie it to a step in the conversation, making data-collection-heavy experiments a challenge. Better would be some type of archive almost like the Pine Aftermath.
  • /dataset allows for some analysis of data, but there is no decent way for it to retrieve data. It does allow for datasets to be recalled, but the user has to type in a time-stamped dataset filename. More practical methods will be needed.
  • I'm leaning towards considering the first iteration of the chat loop as "done" until I do some chemistry and learn what works and what doesn't.
  • From a science perspective - it is critical that a white background be used for the wells. There was unacceptably large variation in the data for a red dye in different wells that seems to have been resolved by placing a white piece of paper under the plate.
  • Some type of pattern on the bottom might be necessary so that we can get white/dark results (R = [samp-dark]/[white-dark]) and then get an absorbance type term with A = -LogR

Chatting about solutions, a digital notebook might be appropriate:

This is a sophisticated architectural challenge. You are moving from a transient, memory-based session (where history is a list in RAM and data is scattered files) to a persistent, stateful document (a "Digital Lab Notebook" file).

To mimic the Pine Research AfterMath archive structure while adhering to modern Agentic principles (LLM-readability), you need a strategy that binds Intent (Chat), Action (Hardware Commands), Result (Data), and Context (Plate State) into a single, portable entity.

Here are four architectural strategies to solve this, ranging from a simple document model to a complex database approach.

docs.title: Strategy 1: The "Digital Notebook" (Chronological Event Stream)
docs.text:
This approach treats the entire experiment as a linear timeline, similar to a Jupyter Notebook or a chat log, but with typed "blocks" or "cells." This is the most natural fit for LLMs, as they process information linearly.

  • The Structure: A single JSON file containing a list of Events.
  • How it works:
    • Block A (Context): The file starts with the Initial State (e.g., Plate Configuration: Reagents in P1-P4, all wells empty).
    • Block B (Interaction): A UserPrompt event ("Run a CV on well A1").
    • Block C (Reasoning): The AgentPlan event (The JSON plan generated by the LLM).
    • Block D (Action & Update): A HardwareAction event. Crucially, this event includes a State Diff.
      • Example: Sidekick dispense 50uL to A1.
      • State Diff: Plate['A1'].volume += 50.
    • Block E (Data): A DataResult event. Instead of saving a file path, you embed the summary (for the LLM) and a reference ID. The raw data can be embedded as a Base64 string (if small) or kept in a parallel folder structure if massive.
  • Handling "Data Mode": When you switch modes, the Agent reads this JSON file. Because the State Diffs are recorded linearly, the Agent can "replay" the history to know exactly what is in Well A1 before performing analysis, without needing a separate database.

docs.title: Strategy 2: The "Project Object" Model (Hierarchical State)
docs.text: This strategy mimics the AfterMath tree structure more directly. Instead of a linear timeline, the file is organized by concepts. This is better if your experiments involve repeated loops or distinct phases.

docs.title: Strategy 3: The "Containerized" Archive (The Zip/HDF5 Approach)
docs.text: If your instrumentation generates heavy data (e.g., high-speed cyclic voltammetry, spectroscopy with thousands of wavelengths), a giant text/JSON file will become too slow to parse and too large for an LLM context window.

docs.title: Strategy 4: The RAG-Enhanced "Living State" (SQLite)
docs.text: If the experiments are long-running (days) or complex, a flat file becomes unwieldy.

docs.title: Recommendation for Your Project Phase
docs.text:
Given you are currently working on the chat.py loop and using Python dictionaries for session_history, Strategy 1 (The Digital Notebook / JSON Stream) is the most practical immediate step.

  1. Serialization: You can easily serialize your existing PlateManager class to a dictionary (implement a to_dict() and from_dict() method).
  2. Context Window: You can feed the last $N$ events of the JSON stream directly to the LLM as context.
  3. Analysis: When switching to DATA Mode, the Agent can look at the JSON file and say, "I see we just ran 3 spectral scans. Here is the summary."

Implementation Logic (Conceptual): Instead of self.session_history.append(...), you would create a SessionManager class that maintains the "Archive" object. * Input: User Prompt. * Process: Append Prompt to Archive -> Execute Plan -> Update Plate State in Archive -> Append Data ID to Archive. * Save: Write archive.json to disk after every turn.

Proposal for creating a stateful experiment manager

docs.title: Phase 1: Data Structure & Schema (The "Archive" Definition)
docs.text:
The biggest shift is treating the log not as a history of text, but as a history of state transitions.

  1. Define the Archive Schema Create a standardized JSON structure that serves as the root file (e.g., experiment_manifest.json). It should have three distinct sections:

    • meta: Experiment name, creation date, author, and initial World Model configuration.
    • timeline: An ordered list of "Transactions" (User requests, Agent plans, Hardware executions, Data capture).
    • state_snapshots: Records of the PlateManager state at specific timestamps (start, post-dispense, end).

  2. Implement the "Transaction" Object Replace the separate plan and step_results lists with a unified transaction block. Every action the agent takes should look like this in the archive: 

    {
  "transaction_id": "tx_101",
  "timestamp": "...",
  "command": "measure",
  "device": "colorimeter",
  "context": { "well": "D3" }, 
  "result": {
    "status": "SUCCESS",
    "dataset_id": "ds_20260219_152546",  // <--- CRITICAL LINK
    "summary": { "max_peak": 540 }       // Lightweight data for AI context
  }
}

docs.title: Phase 2: Logic & execution (The Code Changes)
docs.text:
You need to modify agent_executor.py to capture the links that are currently being dropped.

  1. Capture Registry IDs in Execution Results Currently, registry.register() returns an ID, but step_results only stores the raw payload.

    • Revision: Modify AgentExecutor.run to store the returned ds_id inside the execution log. This creates the "hyperlink" between the chat history and the raw files.

  2. Serialize the PlateManager The PlateManager needs to_dict() and from_dict() methods.

    • Revision: When the AgentExecutor saves the log, it should dump the full state of the PlateManager (volume, contents, history of additions for every well) into the file.
    • Benefit: When you reload the archive, the system knows exactly how much liquid is in every well, preventing overfill errors in future sessions.

  3. Initialize Plate from World Model

    • Revision: Update ai_utils.py / world_building() to allow defining initial well contents (e.g., "A1-A6 = Standards").
    • Revision: When PlateManager initializes, it should read this World Model and pre-fill those wells in its virtual state.

docs.title: Phase 3: Agent Capabilities (The "Digital Twin" Sync)
docs.text:
The Agent needs to be able to update the archive even when it isn't moving motors.

  1. Create "Virtual" Commands for State Updates In your example, you told the Agent: "There are solutions in D3...". The Agent planned a measurement, but the PlateManager remained unaware of the liquid.
    • Revision: Add a system device with a command update_well_state.
    • Workflow: The Planner realizes it needs to update the mental model and generates a plan like:
      1. system.update_well_state(well="D3", content="Unknown Soln A")
      2. sidekick.to_well("D3")
      3. colorimeter.measure()
    • Result: The archive now formally records that D3 is not empty.

docs.title: Phase 4: Storage Mechanism (The "File Format")
docs.text:
To fully mimic AfterMath, you want a single file that is easy to move but contains complex data.

  1. Implement a Zip-based Container Format (.alif) Instead of a folder full of loose JSONs and CSVs:
    • Revision: Create a SessionManager class that wraps Python's zipfile module.
    • Structure: 
      experiment_01.alif  (Zip Archive)
├── manifest.json   (The Chat/State/Timeline Log)
└── data/           (Folder containing raw datasets)
    ├── ds_2026...json
    └── ds_2026...csv
    • Action: When the user types /log or /quit, the system writes the manifest, gathers the referenced data files, and zips them into one portable file.

docs.title: Summary Checklist for Implementation
docs.text:
• [ ] Add to_dict serialization to PlateManager.
  • [ ] Update World Model JSON schema to support initial plate content definitions.
  • [ ] Modify AgentExecutor to save dataset_id in the step results.
  • [ ] Create a SessionArchive class to handle the JSON schema generation.
  • [ ] Implement the Zip-container logic to bundle the JSON manifest with raw data files.

The environent configuration via the world model is not being fed to the agent. Planner.py specifically filters for reagents and something along the lines of the following is needed. This is separate from the archive structure. A simple project to inject environment configuration is needed - this will probably require a change to the world model schema (clearly indicating what information should be added to context) and planner.py

docs.title: ENVIRONMENT CONFIGURATION
docs.text:
Waste Location: {"x": 5, "y": -5.3} Max Well Volume: 250 uL

In one interaction, I asked to move to the well, measure it, dispense some liquid and measure again. The agent had trouble recognizing that sensor/pump offsets required moves in between measurements and dispensing. It eventually worked within the chat, but this might require either better usage notes or a dispense_at_well that combines the well location, pump, and volume.

For fun, I asked Gemini to create a lab report based on the chat log 152615 from today. Here is the result. I think it should be feasible to generate a script that will take the chat log and the report rubric to generate a summary.

Experiment Title: Automated Reflectance Spectrophotometry of Aqueous Food Coloring Solutions

Date: February 19, 2026 Session ID: Chat_Session Operator: AI Laboratory Controller (Sidekick Arm / Colorimeter)

docs.title: 1. Objective
docs.text: The objective of this experiment was to utilize an automated robotic arm equipped with a reflectance colorimeter to measure the spectral properties of four unknown solutions located in a 96-well plate and correlate the spectral data with the known sample contents (Blue, Yellow, Green, and Red food coloring).

docs.title: 2. Materials & Methods
docs.text:
• Hardware: Sidekick Robotic Arm, On-board Colorimeter (Reflectance Mode).
  • Reagents: Four colored solutions dispensed into a 96-well plate:
    • Well D3: Blue
    • Well D6: Yellow
    • Well D9: Green
    • Well D12: Red
  • Procedure:
    1. The robotic arm was initialized and homed to ensure positional accuracy.
    2. The arm moved sequentially to wells D3, D6, D9, and D12.
    3. At each well, the arm centered the colorimeter end-effector.
    4. A measurement sequence was triggered (LED illumination followed by sensor reading) to capture light reflectance counts across eight color channels.

docs.title: 3. Results
docs.text:
The following raw reflectance data (measured in counts) was obtained for each well. Higher counts indicate higher reflectance at that specific wavelength band.

Channel Well D3 (Blue) Well D6 (Yellow) Well D9 (Green) Well D12 (Red) Violet 2,355 3,472 1,689 2,250 Indigo 24,188 7,605 6,037 7,635 Blue 29,494 13,125 12,297 11,293 Cyan 24,579 26,496 21,444 11,672 Green 18,516 55,358 18,017 20,110 Yellow 14,945 57,120 12,568 35,014 Orange 17,744 60,969 15,100 46,807 Red 21,601 40,150 19,057 30,507

docs.title: 4. Analysis & Discussion
docs.text:
Well D3 (Blue Sample): The spectral profile exhibits a distinct peak in the Blue (29,494) channel, with strong secondary support in Cyan and Indigo. Long-wavelength channels (Yellow, Orange) show significantly reduced reflectance compared to the shorter wavelengths. This profile is consistent with a blue solution, which reflects blue light and absorbs yellow/orange wavelengths.

Well D6 (Yellow Sample): This sample produced the highest overall intensity counts in the experiment, suggesting a bright or highly reflective solution. The spectrum is dominated by a peak in Orange (60,969) and Yellow (57,120), with very strong Green reflectance. Conversely, reflectance in the Violet and Indigo channels is minimal (<8,000), indicating strong absorption of blue/violet light, which is characteristic of yellow pigment.

Well D9 (Green Sample): The data for this well is generally lower in intensity than the yellow sample, suggesting a darker solution or lower concentration. The spectrum shows a primary peak in Cyan (21,444), which bridges the Blue and Green channels. While the "Green" channel count (18,017) is moderate, the suppression of Indigo/Violet (low reflectance) and the relative strength of Cyan/Green compared to the Blue channel supports the identification of a green solution.

Well D12 (Red Sample): The spectrum shows a clear trend toward longer wavelengths. The highest peaks are observed in Orange (46,807) and Yellow (35,014), followed by Red. Crucially, the short wavelengths (Blue, Cyan, Indigo) are significantly suppressed (all roughly ~11k or lower). This absorption of the blue-green spectrum and reflection of the yellow-red spectrum confirms the solution is red.

docs.title: 5. Conclusion
docs.text:
The robotic colorimeter successfully differentiated the four solutions. The spectral data correlates accurately with the visual ground truth: * D3 displayed a Blue-dominant spectrum. * D6 displayed a Yellow/Orange-dominant spectrum. * D9 displayed a Cyan/Green profile. * D12 displayed a Red/Orange-dominant spectrum.

The system functioned within nominal parameters, successfully moving to all targets and returning valid data structures for all measurements.

docs.title: Calibration 260206
docs.text:
These notes document the sidekick calibration branch where I worked on understanding better the forward kinematics and how the equations could be optimized for better positioning over the 96 well plate. The end result was improved (but not ideal) positioning through the use of an error minimization routine that used the steps and origin offset as adjustable parameters. The assumption made here is that the end stops do not accurately represent 0 and 1600 steps for m1 and m2, respectively. The addition of this fixed step offset seems to have improved things. At some point, a rotation in addition to translation might be needed. However, to do this, a better data collection approach will be needed by comparing the actual and predicted positions. Perhaps using a pump in the center of the end effector and depositing a single drop at a position might provide useful information.

Completed some housecleaning (removed calibration routines) and started improved transect data collection that includes multi-channel data collection and visualization. Next step is either (a) create a more complete template with multiple spots for analysis or (b) continue using filled wells. Either way must result in a method that can be implemented to improve IK. See chat [!] Calibrate Arm Using Numerical Optimization for some strategies.

Working on sidekick calibration. joint_cross_search seems to be good at finding a minimum to pinpoint the center of a well, but using this information in a calibration is problematic. Next step is to try different calibration methods, possibly with a template that has a few more data points.

Six point data collection providing the predicted and actual steps

well m1_pred m2_pred m1_act m2_act A1 335 887 366 892 H1 131 1178 137 1171 C5 552 1113 564 1116 F8 546 1308 543 1306 A12 987 1486 971 1469 H12 518 1516 501 1510

Mathematica code to convert angles to coordinates 

pt[t1_, t2_, l1_, l3_] := Module[{x, y},
  x = l1 Cos[t1 Degree] - l3 Cos[t2 Degree];
  y = l1 Sin[t1 Degree] - l3 Sin[t2 Degree];
  {x, y}
  ]
And converting steps to angles is multiplying by 0.9 (step angle degrees) and diviging by 8 (microsteps)

Analysis of these data with step_analysis.py reveals that the m1 home is actually at -33, the m2 home is at 1610, and there is an effective axis separation of 5.607 mm. I'm skeptical about the axis separation, especially one that big. Time to try implementing that.

Several days later, two points coincide with above table (one was exact, the other differed by 1 step in one dimension). More exploration of forward kinematics using this website as a resource. Mathematica code stored in temp (5-bar-forward-kinematics.nb).

Using mathematica to fit the forward kinetics equation with predicted (robot) cartesian coordinates and actual (well plate - A1 -> 0,0) coordinates using a translation offset and step offset seems to have created an improved calibration.

Additional optimization in calibration is needed. However, the four corner wells can be reached by each of the pumps. This might be good enough for now.

Mathematica code used to describe forward kinematics and the optimization. ``` (link-center points) xyl[q1_, a1_] := {a1 Cos[q1], a1 Sin[q1]} xyr[q2_, a2_, c_] := {c + a2 Cos[q2], a2 Sin[q2]}

(deltas and distance between centers) dxdy[q1_, q2_, a1_, a2_, c_] := xyr[q2, a2, c] - xyl[q1, a1] dist[q1_, q2_, a1_, a2_, c_] := Module[{dx, dy}, {dx, dy} = dxdy[q1, q2, a1, a2, c]; Sqrt[dx^2 + dy^2]]

(scalars a and h) asc[q1_, q2_, a1_, a2_, a3_, a4_, c_] := Module[{d = dist[q1, q2, a1, a2, c]}, (a3^2 - a4^2 + d^2)/(2 d)] hsc[q1_, q2_, a1_, a2_, a3_, a4_, c_] := Module[{a = asc[q1, q2, a1, a2, a3, a4, c]}, Sqrt[Max[0, a3^2 - a^2]]]

(base point on the line of centers) xbase[q1_, q2_, a1_, a2_, a3_, a4_, c_] := Module[{xl, dx, d, a}, xl = xyl[q1, a1]; {dx, d} = {dxdy[q1, q2, a1, a2, c][[1]], dist[q1, q2, a1, a2, c]}; a = asc[q1, q2, a1, a2, a3, a4, c]; xl[[1]] + (a/d) dx] ybase[q1_, q2_, a1_, a2_, a3_, a4_, c_] := Module[{yl, dy, d, a}, yl = xyl[q1, a1]; {dy, d} = {dxdy[q1, q2, a1, a2, c][[2]], dist[q1, q2, a1, a2, c]}; a = asc[q1, q2, a1, a2, a3, a4, c]; yl[[2]] + (a/d) dy]

(forward kinematics:returns {xd,yd};s=\ [PlusMinus]1 selects assembly mode) FK[q1_, q2_, a1_, a2_, a3_, a4_, c_, s_ : 1] := Module[{dx, dy, d, xb, yb, h}, {dx, dy} = dxdy[q1, q2, a1, a2, c]; d = dist[q1, q2, a1, a2, c]; xb = xbase[q1, q2, a1, a2, a3, a4, c]; yb = ybase[q1, q2, a1, a2, a3, a4, c]; h = hsc[q1, q2, a1, a2, a3, a4, c]; If[d == 0, Return[$Failed]];(degenerate){xb - s (h/d) dy, yb + s (h/d) dx}]

(forward kinematics with end effector) pred[m1_, m2_] := FK[m1 Pi/1600, m2 Pi/1600, 7, 3, 3, 7, 0, -1] - 13 {Cos[m2 Pi/1600], Sin[m2 Pi/1600]} // N pred[366, 892]

model[m1_, m2_] := {xoffset, yoffset} + FK[(m1 + m1e) Pi/1600, (m2 + m2e) Pi/1600, 7, 3, 3, 7, 0, -1] - (13) {Cos[(m2 + m2e) Pi/1600], Sin[(m2 + m2e) Pi/1600]} obs = MapThread[ pred, {{366, 137, 564, 543, 971, 501}, {892, 1171, 1116, 1306, 1469, 1510}}]; actual = {{0, 0}, {6.3, 0}, {1.8, 3.6}, {4.5, 6.3}, {0, 9.9}, {6.3, 9.9}};

( error function is the sum of the square of the differences, \ adding the results of x and y at each point ) Total [(#[[1]]^2 + #[[2]]^2) & /@ (actual - obs)]

( optimization routing ) FindMinimum[Total [(#[[1]]^2 + #[[2]]^2) & /@ (actual - MapThread[ model, {{366, 137, 564, 543, 971, 501}, {892, 1171, 1116, 1306, 1469, 1510}}] )], {{xoffset, -7}, {yoffset, 5}, {m1e, 2}, {m2e, 25}}]

docs.title: Experiment Record: Acid-Base Titration with Universal Indicator
docs.text: Date: 2026-02-20 Session ID: 2026-02-20T12:23:20.183711 Operator: Sidekick AI Controller / Human Supervisor

docs.title: 1. Objective
docs.text: To perform a colorimetric titration of an acetate buffer with sodium hydroxide, monitoring the pH changes using universal indicator and a colorimeter. The experiment aims to observe the spectral changes associated with pH variation.

docs.title: 2. Materials & Equipment
docs.text:
• Hardware: Sidekick Robotic Arm, Colorimeter
  • Reagents:
    • 0.1 M Acetic Acid (from p1)
    • 0.1 M Sodium Hydroxide (from p2)
    • Universal Indicator (from p3)
    • Water (from p4)

docs.title: 3. Procedure
docs.text:
• The robotic arm was homed at the start of the session.
  • The following liquid handling actions were performed:
    • Well D6 was filled with 700 µL of 0.1 M acetic acid (p1), 500 µL of 0.1 M sodium hydroxide (p2), 500 µL of universal indicator (p3), and 500 µL of water (p4).
    • Well A1 was filled with 100 µL of 0.1 M acetic acid (p1), followed by 100 µL of 0.1 M sodium hydroxide (p2), and 10 µL of universal indicator (p3).
    • A series of wells (C1-C12) were prepared:
      • Well C1 was filled with 200 µL of 0.1 M acetic acid (p1) and 10 µL of universal indicator (p3).
      • Wells C2-C11 were filled with a gradient of 0.1 M acetic acid (p1) ranging from 20 µL to 180 µL in 20 µL increments, followed by a gradient of 0.1 M sodium hydroxide (p2) ranging from 180 µL down to 20 µL in 20 µL decrements, and finally 10 µL of universal indicator (p3) in each.
      • Well C12 was filled with 200 µL of 0.1 M sodium hydroxide (p2) and 10 µL of universal indicator (p3).
    • Well F6 was filled with 200 µL of water (p4) as a control.
  • The colorimeter settings were adjusted: LED intensity was set to 10 mA, and gain was set to 128. Subsequently, reflectance spectra were measured for wells F6, and C1 through C12.

docs.title: 4. Observations & Results
docs.text:
The colorimeter's LED was initially off. After adjusting settings to an intensity of 10 mA and a gain of 128, spectral measurements were taken.

The following reflectance spectral data was collected:

Well Violet (counts) Indigo (counts) Blue (counts) Cyan (counts) Green (counts) Yellow (counts) Orange (counts) Red (counts) F6 4025 26136 33147 41109 51506 50246 54804 34206 C1 2164 9916 14043 16712 23995 33365 42362 26832 C2 2043 11247 16488 19793 21421 20163 26568 26082 C3 2139 11884 17984 21736 23011 20077 25595 25950 C4 2102 11490 17678 21025 22491 19398 24686 26172 C5 2021 10588 16640 19734 21420 18536 23642 24016 C6 2004 10369 16134 19533 21047 18527 23546 24938 C7 2558 9298 15272 23376 33797 36575 42766 29666 C8 2458 8803 13285 16942 26199 36455 45177 28607 C9 2460 9123 13054 15640 24533 38211 49063 30737 C10 2449 9108 13005 15145 23743 37292 49312 30482 C11 2653 9982 14226 16455 26112 41385 54277 33889 C12 1968 10364 15534 18356 20078 17961 24241 25382
  • Trends: A general trend observed across wells C1-C12 was significantly higher reflectance in the yellow and orange regions of the spectrum compared to other wavelengths, particularly violet and indigo. This pattern suggests a common characteristic of the solutions in these wells related to light absorption or scattering. There were notable variations between the wells, likely corresponding to the different ratios of acetic acid and sodium hydroxide.
  • Operational Anomalies: None were recorded.

docs.title: 5. Conclusions
docs.text: The experimental setup and liquid handling procedures were successfully executed by the robotic system. The colorimetric measurements indicate distinct spectral profiles for the prepared solutions, with a pronounced peak in the yellow-orange range. This spectral data is consistent with the presence of universal indicator and the varying chemical environments created by the titration series.

docs.title: 6. Next Steps
docs.text:
• Analyze the spectral data further to correlate specific wavelength intensities with pH values or buffer concentrations.
  • Perform additional titrations with varying starting buffer concentrations or different acids/bases to build a comprehensive spectral library for pH indication.
  • Investigate the spectral signature of the control (Well F6) to establish a baseline.
  • Consider performing a pH measurement on a subset of the wells using a standard pH meter to validate the colorimetric estimations.

docs.title: Chem1 260220
docs.text:
Performed a "real" experiment today - a titration of acetate buffer with sodium hydroxide.

The experiment demonstrated

Here is the report generated by gemini 3 Pro, so we are seeing a little bit of a model skill level difference

Experiment Record: Automated Colorimetric Titration of Acetic Acid

Date: February 20, 2026 Session ID: Chat-Session-20260220_125201 Operator: Sidekick AI Controller

docs.title: 1. Objective
docs.text: To perform an automated conductometric titration of a weak acid (0.1 M Acetic Acid) with a strong base (0.1 M Sodium Hydroxide) using a 96-well plate format. The reaction is monitored colorimetrically using a Universal Indicator and an arm-mounted reflectance colorimeter to detect pH-dependent spectral shifts.

docs.title: 2. Materials & Equipment
docs.text:
• Hardware: Sidekick Robotic Arm, Colorimeter End-Effector.
  • Reagents:
    • p1: 0.1 M Acetic Acid ($CH_3COOH$) - Starting pH ~4.3
    • p2: 0.1 M Sodium Hydroxide ($NaOH$)
    • p3: Universal Indicator
    • p4: Water (Blank)

docs.title: 3. Procedure
docs.text:
3.1. System Priming & Verification Prior to the main experiment, the system performed homing and priming operations. A test mixture was dispensed in well D6 and a functional verification of the 1:1 neutralization was performed in well A1 (100 $\mu$L Acid + 100 $\mu$L Base + 10 $\mu$L Indicator).

3.2. Titration Gradient Preparation (Row C) A 12-point cross-titration series was prepared in wells C1 through C12 with a constant total volume of 200 $\mu$L per well (excluding indicator).

  • Acid Dispense (p1):
    • C1: 200 $\mu$L (Pure Acid)
    • C2–C11: Linear gradient increasing from 20 $\mu$L to 180 $\mu$L in 20 $\mu$L increments.
    • C12: 0 $\mu$L
  • Base Dispense (p2):
    • C1: 0 $\mu$L
    • C2–C11: Linear gradient decreasing from 180 $\mu$L to 20 $\mu$L in 20 $\mu$L decrements.
    • C12: 200 $\mu$L (Pure Base)
  • Indicator Dispense (p3):
    • 10 $\mu$L of Universal Indicator was added to all active wells (A1, C1–C12).

3.3. Measurement * Blanking: A water blank (200 $\mu$L) was prepared in well F6 and measured to establish a baseline. * Sensor Settings: The colorimeter was configured with LED Intensity: 10 mA and Gain: 128x. * Data Acquisition: Reflectance spectra (Violet, Indigo, Blue, Cyan, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red) were captured sequentially for wells C1 through C12.

docs.title: 4. Observations & Results
docs.text:
Spectral Data Summary (Raw Counts): The reflectance data reveals two distinct spectral profiles corresponding to the acidic and basic/neutral regions of the titration.

  • Acid-Dominant Phase (Wells C1, C8–C11):
    • These wells exhibited high reflectance in the Orange (42,000–54,000) and Yellow (33,000–41,000) channels, with significant Red reflectance.
    • This is consistent with the Red/Orange color expected of Universal Indicator in acidic conditions (pH < 5).
  • Base-Dominant/Transition Phase (Wells C2–C7, C12):
    • These wells showed significantly lower reflectance in the warm channels (Orange ~23,000–26,000).
    • The suppression of the Orange/Yellow reflection suggests a shift toward the Blue/Purple end of the spectrum (indicating pH > 7), where longer wavelengths are absorbed rather than reflected.

Notable Transition: A sharp discontinuity in the raw count intensity was observed between Well C7 (120 $\mu$L Acid / 80 $\mu$L Base) and Well C8 (140 $\mu$L Acid / 60 $\mu$L Base). The Orange channel counts jumped from ~24,000 (C7) to ~42,000 (C8).

  • Note: The equivalence point for Acetic Acid + NaOH occurs at a basic pH (~8.7). Well C6 (100:100 mix) theoretically represents the stoichiometric equivalence point, but data suggests the colorimetric transition in reflectance intensity (or the specific sensitivity of this sensor setup) becomes most distinct at higher acid concentrations in this dataset.

docs.title: 5. Conclusions
docs.text: The automated platform successfully generated a titration curve and differentiated between acidic and basic solutions based on spectral reflectance. The raw data indicates that the "warm" color channels (Orange/Yellow/Red) are the most sensitive discriminators for this specific indicator/sensor combination, showing high signal in acidic states and signal attenuation in basic states.

docs.title: 6. Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Data Processing: Normalize raw counts against the F6 Water Blank to calculate Absorbance or Reflectance %.
  2. Resolution Enhancement: Repeat the experiment with finer volume increments (e.g., 5 $\mu$L) between the C6 and C8 range to better resolve the colorimetric endpoint.
  3. Wavelength Analysis: Plot the ratio of (Blue Channel / Orange Channel) vs. Volume of NaOH to linearize the color change for pH calculation.

docs.title: Journaling the colorimeter design
docs.text: purpose: The purpose of this document is to log the experience of creating a new firmware package with the help of AI. I am using the socratic_design_guidelines provided in the documentation and will hopefully provide an honest depiction of how the process works.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text: Create a colorimeter firmware module using an Adafruit Feather M4 Express and Adafruit AS7341 10-channel color sensor breakout.

docs.title: Additional tools and resources
docs.text:
AI implemented will be Gemini (2.5 Pro) via the Google AI Studio interface.

Adafruit documentation for sensor is here

docs.title: setting up sensor
docs.text:
Prior to SDL framework, I'm checking that the sensor works. two libraries are needed adafruit_as7341 and adafruit_register, both of which are in the standard CP bundle

The documentation for this sensor is incomplete. For example, there is a white LED on board but the documentation does not describe its use. (set sensor.led to True/False and the default sensor.led_current is 4). There are flicker detection routines and apparently a GPIO and interrupt for triggering based on measurements. The first generation of this device will read all channels, individual channels, turn the light on/off, adjust the light brightness, and adjust the gain.

I would like to make color/wavelength relationships easy, but not sure how to implement that. - 415 violet - 445 indigo - 480 blue - 515 cyan - 555 green - 590 yellow - 630 orange - 680 red - xxx clear - yyy nir

The firmware design should not be overly complicated give that the sensor requires minimal setup, provides little diagnostic information, and doesn't have a lot of "states". I have decided to add a placeholder state for more advanced collection methods but since I don't know what I want here, it is not completely defined in the template.

I pasted the AI assistant instrument design markdown into gemini, added the current software stack (using all-in-one) and the socratic_colorimeter.md

Here is the response from Gemini 2.5 Pro

Excellent. I am ready to begin. As an expert firmware developer specializing in this framework, I will follow the prescribed process precisely.

### **Step 1: Analysis of Provided Materials**

I have thoroughly analyzed the provided software stack and the new instrument design spec sheet.

**Software Stack Analysis:**

*   **Architecture:** The framework is built on a unified `StateMachine` class. Each instrument is a self-contained module with a single `machine` instance.
*   **Core Files:** The standard structure is `__init__.py` (assembly), `states.py` (behavior), and `handlers.py` (commands).
*   **State Management:** States are distinct classes inheriting from `State`. The framework provides common states like `GenericIdle` and `GenericError`, which should be used to reduce boilerplate. `GenericIdle` is particularly important as it handles periodic telemetry and command listening via the `listen_for_instructions` helper.
*   **Command Handling:** The `StateMachine` holds a dictionary of commands. Handlers are simple functions that take `machine` and `payload` as arguments. They are responsible for changing flags, triggering state transitions, and sending response messages.
*   **Configuration:** There is a clear separation between static hardware configuration (in a `CONFIG` dictionary, attached to `machine.config`) and dynamic runtime variables (in `machine.flags`).
*   **Communication:** `CircuitPythonPostman` is the standard for device-side communication. All messages are `Message` objects serialized to JSON.

**New Instrument Spec Sheet Analysis (Colorimeter):**

*   **Device:** An AS7341 10-channel I2C color sensor.
*   **Purpose:** Measure light across different wavelengths, control an onboard LED, and adjust sensor gain.
*   **Configuration:** The spec provides a `CONFIG` dictionary with I2C pins, default gain, and default LED intensity. I note a minor syntax error (missing commas), which I will correct.
*   **Commands:** The command set is clear and maps well to the expected capabilities of the AS7341 sensor library. The arguments and success conditions are defined.
*   **States:** An `Initialize` state is required for I2C setup. A placeholder `Collecting` state is requested. The standard `Idle` and `Error` states will also be necessary.
*   **Telemetry:** The requirement is to periodically report the LED status.

The analysis is complete. I will now proceed to the critical second step.

---

### **Step 2: Ask Clarifying Questions**

Based on my analysis of the spec sheet and the software architecture, I have identified several areas that require clarification to ensure the firmware is robust and behaves as expected. Please provide answers to the following questions:

1.  **`read` Command Specifics:** The `read` command's argument is "channel (color)". The spec sheet log mentions specific wavelengths and colors (e.g., "415 violet"). For a user-friendly API, should the host send a simple color string (e.g., `"violet"`, `"blue"`, `"green"`)? If so, could you confirm the complete list of desired channel strings? My proposed list is: `"violet"`, `"indigo"`, `"blue"`, `"cyan"`, `"green"`, `"yellow"`, `"orange"`, `"red"`, `"clear"`, and `"nir"`.

2.  **Invalid Argument Handling:** For the `set_gain` and `led_intensity` commands, the spec defines valid argument ranges. If the host sends a value outside of these ranges (e.g., a gain of `10` or an intensity of `15`), what is the desired behavior? My recommendation, based on the framework's design, is to reject the command and reply with a `PROBLEM` message containing an error description. Is this correct?

3.  **LED Command Argument Format:** The `led` command accepts a "boolean" argument. To ensure robust parsing, what specific format should be expected from the host? Common options are JSON booleans (`true`/`false`), integers (`1`/`0`), or strings (`"on"`/`"off"`). Please specify the preferred format.

4.  **Telemetry Content:** The requirement is to "send led status" as periodic telemetry. Should this telemetry packet include only the on/off state of the LED, or should it also include the current LED intensity setting for a more complete status update?

5.  **Hardware Library Assumption:** I will proceed under the assumption that the firmware should use the standard Adafruit CircuitPython library for this sensor (`adafruit_as7341`). Please confirm if this is the correct library to use.

6.  **`Collecting` State Trigger:** The `Collecting` state is currently a placeholder. To create a useful skeleton, how will this state eventually be entered? Will it be triggered by a new command (e.g., `start_collection`)? For now, I will create the state class, but it will not be reachable. Is this the correct approach?

I will await your answers before proceeding to Step 3.

After answering the questions and eating a piece of fruit, I copy pasted the three files that were generated into the firmware directory. I noticed that the template does not provide a suggestion to update firmware_db, so I will make it PID 813 and VID 808 The deploy.py function doesn't work well but the batch file (using xcopy) seems to do a better job.

Once the deployment issues were taken care of, the colorimeter started reporting telemetry data and responded to the read_all command.

I'm pleasantly surprised that the basic operation works fine. I think the template needs to provide some additional detail to the AI about how payloads, functions, and arguments are to be formatted, but since I haven't given that topic sufficient thought, it is no surprise that the AI provides different approaches.

docs.title: [2026-01-28] Development documentation lacking
docs.text: Context: Returning to the project after a one month(+) hiatus. Nothing in the documentation, git repository, or dev log provides meaningful information on where I was when the project was set aside. Issues: There is a (re)learning curve to determine what was working and what wasn't. There is no resource such as a plan to refer to. Commentary - A plan would be nice to have. An end goal for the next stable version and then a series of projects to achieve that state. - I'm creating some un-tracked files (CURRENT_STATUS and BRANCH_GOALS) to assist in documenting progress and tasks. - I plan to branch three tasks: sidekick calibration, mcp implementation, agentic chat loop. The order is important: AI is useless if hardware is failing; MCP impelementation drives logic decisions in the agentic chat loop.

docs.title: [2025-11-12] Functional ai planner(?)
docs.text: Context: Working towards AI operation of the sidekick so that it can generate a set of instructions from a human readable prompt Issues: Three broad categories of challenges: the AI cannot do math, the AI doesn't remember the hardware state, the AI doesn't fully understand the effects of the commands Commentary - Original planning runs would occasionally remember that to_well has a pump argument which is necessary to run prior to dispense. - Unrelated to the planner, there is still a calibration problem and A1 is not being reached properly when one of the pumps is requested. It appears that all pumps are going to the same (incorrect) location - Related to ^^^ the AI is capable of correctly interpreting the prompt: "starting from well E6 ..." - Deterministic python tools for doing chemistry math will be needed, but that is going to be a significant undertaking that requires some thought. - The current approach is to provide more information to the AI from the firmware itself. Each instruction has additional useage_notes and effects keys that are passed on to the AI prompt - With ^^^ we obtain a near-perfect execution of a prompt to create three solutions of varying volumetric ratios. In this case, a math tool isn't needed if the proper phrasing is used. - Creating a planner repository in docs for sequences that are worth keeping.

docs.title: [2025-10-31] how to handle send_problem
docs.text: Context: As handlers become more sophisticated and rely on helper functions, there needs to be some protocol for determining who gets to send messages to host Issues: The send_problem function is being used in helper functions, and it may be more appropriate for these functions to only log information to the machine and let the handler deal with communication Action: Review sidekick functions and consider refactoring.
